policy_statement: Adopt an ordinance requiring all applicable new buildings to be fully electric with no natural gas hookups, with affordability considerations and supporting measures to defray first costs.
mechanism_description: Adopt an ordinance requiring applicable new buildings to be fully electric with no natural gas hookups, avoiding on-site fossil fuel combustion and associated emissions while supporting affordability through measures that defray first costs.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy system choice (all-electric) → ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ direct GHG emissions (and often ↓ lifecycle emissions as grids decarbonize) → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: New-building design and equipment selection shifts from gas-fired end uses (furnaces/boilers, water heaters, stoves, dryers) to electric end uses (heat pumps, electric resistance/induction, etc.), typically with no gas service connection.  
2) Mediating physical process: Eliminating combustion prevents CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions at the building site; remaining energy demand is met by electricity whose emissions intensity depends on the grid and procurement, with emissions generally declining over time as grids decarbonize.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in operational (Scope 1) building emissions and frequently overall lifecycle GHG emissions, avoiding long-lived gas infrastructure lock-in.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building operational GHG emissions by avoiding on-site fossil fuel combustion in new construction.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism directly targets and reduces GHG emissions by removing on-site fossil fuel combustion in new buildings (operational mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve energy efficiency and indoor air quality and can interact with grid decarbonization, the central, guaranteed effect across policies using this mechanism is eliminating direct combustion emissions and gas lock-in—best categorized as Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency or Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity supply is carbon-intensive, near-term total emissions reductions could be smaller, but the mechanism still primarily functions via combustion avoidance and enables deeper reductions as the grid cleans; affordability elements do not change the dominant climate pathway.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any “all-electric new buildings/no gas hookups” ordinance shares the same primary mitigation pathway of avoiding on-site fossil fuel use; specifics (scope, exemptions, timelines) alter magnitude, not category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., electrification, building decarbonization, equity/affordability co-benefits) depends on the individual policy text and implementation details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include jurisdictions with very carbon-intensive grids (reducing near-term net benefits), exemptions for certain end uses (diluting effect), or policies framed mainly as affordability/health measures; however, the canonical mechanism remains operational GHG mitigation via on-site fossil fuel avoidance.
key_indicators: "Adopt an ordinance"; "requiring all applicable new buildings to be fully electric"; "no natural gas hookups"; "supporting measures (financial support, technical assistance, or other incentives) to defray potential additional first costs"; "maintain housing affordability"
co_benefits: Housing affordability/equity (affordable housing considerations; defray first costs); improved indoor air quality and health from reduced combustion pollutants; avoided gas infrastructure lock-in/costs
instance_edge_case_notes: Mixed instrument package: primary classification is the ordinance (mandate), while the referenced "financial support/technical assistance/other incentives" are complementary enabling measures but not the main policy action described.
